items.item: Information on all defects and damages for the last 5-year period

Documents issued and/or approved by the Register, including all the latest reports of the ship survey performed by the Register or IACS, all information for the master and instructions regarding matters regulated by the Register rules (strength, ballasting, shipment of special cargoes, etc.)

Ship technical documentation, i.e. drawings, diagrams, descriptions, service instructions, files or passports, normative and operational documentation on ship, machinery and arrangements. In case the shipowner is unable to provide the technical documentation in full, measures shall be taken to provide the required information with calculations, test results and drawings made, if necessary

For all the items of the Register technical supervision (machinery, boilers, pressure vessels and other equipment and outfit), there shall be available onboard the certificates of conformity or manufacturer's documents according to the rules and also, if necessary, the proper operational and repair documentation for products
Log books and other documents reflecting operation of ship and machinery, arrangements, equipment subject to the Register supervision, malfunctions occurred to them and their elimination
Technical documentation on repair/modernization/conversion approved by PhRS, whatever is applicable (approved documentation shall be on board a ship before the commencement of works)
Information that confirms that new structures, spare parts, materials or products on board the ship do not contain asbestos
Planned scope of survey in accordance with PhRS normative documents.
The scheme for placing the ship for this docking should be available for PhRS surveyor prior to commencement survey
Necessity of thickness measurements.
Conditions for survey provided by shipowner in accordance with PhRS requirements, including:
all the ship's spaces shall be made accessible for the survey;
all items shall be in good working order except for cases of repairs and accidents;
in necessary cases access, opening-up and/or dismantling shall be provided;
safe access to structures in accordance with PhRS requirements and acceptable to the PhRS surveyor should be provided;
cargo holds, tanks and spaces are to be gas free and properly ventilated;
prior to entering a tank, void or enclosed space, it is to be verified that the atmosphere in the tank is free from hazardous gas and contains sufficient oxygen;
holds, tanks and other spaces subject to survey are to be cleaned, free from water, scale, dirt, oil residues etc.;
safe lighting should be provided for the efficient conduct of the survey;
protective means should be given to PhRS surveyor: helmet, gloves, glasses, noise protection arrangements and if necessary protective clothing and safety shoes;

where soft coatings have been applied, safe access is to be provided to verify the effectiveness of the coating and to carry out an assessment of the conditions of internal structures which may include spot removal of the coating. When safe access cannot be provided, the soft coating is to be removed.

All changes and additional measures/decisions, accepted during survey, shall be agreed with PhRS surveyor and information about accepted and agreed alterations/decisions shall be registered in relevant PhRS reporting documents.
During survey PhRS surveyor may require changing of the following:
scope of survey, thickness measurements, including increasing of it;
quality and extent of preparation of technical supervision items to examination, gauging etc.;
extent of repair upon the results of survey, fault detection, defects parameters gauging etc.
extent of tests upon the results of survey, repair etc..

The surveyor has the right to reject execution of the survey if the ship or item of the survey is inadequately prepared for survey execution, and in case of the defects revealed, which affect survey and/or test safety, the surveyor shall withdraw the survey. At that the report with the comments on the PhRS surveyor's decision shall be submitted to the ship.

The Register may invite experts in shipbuilding and mechanical engineering to resolve issues regarding new types of ships, machinery and arrangements, as well as for resolution of extraordinary important problems, which occur during repairs, accident investigations and in other cases

Organizations and individuals involved in operation, repairs, modernization or conversion of ships subject to the Register technical supervision shall provide support for the surveyor in his technical supervision of repair works, manufacture and testing of materials and products for ships.

During surveys, repairs, modernization, conversion and operation of ships the shipowners or duly authorized their representatives, as well as organizations involved in the relevant process (for instance, designers, shipyards, ship repair yards, service suppliers etc., whatever is applicable) shall follow the requirements of the Register rules.

In order to assist surveyors during the ship survey, the master and/or chief engineer in charge of the ship submission shall assign their mates responsible for operation and maintenance of the ship part to be submitted, which are familiar with all the faults, defects and damages within the time period after the previous survey and with their elimination.

Shipowners, organizations and firms shall ensure all the necessary conditions for quality and safe survey or testing of items. All the ship machinery, equipment and instruments shall be in working order (except cases of repairs and accidents); ladders, scaffoldings and other arrangements for surveys of ships shall be in a safe operating condition, and also all the safely precautions for the survey performed shall be made, in particular, regarding internal surveys of tanks, boilers and pressure vessels, as well as examination of structures at height, crankcases, etc.

Responsibility for timely submission of ships and ship's items of technical supervision for surveys and timely implementation of the surveyor's requirements and comments set out in the Register documents (reports, certificates, etc.) stays with the shipowner and the ship administration. Performance of testing, measurements and testing for defects is the responsibility of the shipowner or the works repairing the ship.
Ensuring the safety of survey, tests of ship’s items and ship as a whole is a joint responsibility of shipowner and organization(s), carrying out repair, modernization, conversion etc.
Fulfillment of applicable safety requirements, including fire safety, during performance of repair / modernization / conversion works etc.
Ensuring structural integrity, strength, watertightness of hull structures, efficiency of corrosion prevention system etc.
Before commencement of works submission to PhRS surveyor of materials with appropriate certificates, required for repair / modernization / conversion.
Submission of repair works / modernization / conversion works carrying out to PhRS surveyor with appropriate reports of organization, carrying out such works.
Submission of repair works / modernization / conversion works carrying out to PhRS surveyor with appropriate reports of organization, carrying out such works.
Performance of necessary tests upon completion of repair / modernization / conversion according to PhRS Rules requirements.

Immediate notification of the Register / PhRS surveyor regarding all cases of fractures/cracks, deformations, fires and floods, resulting in damage to the hull structures, mechanisms, equipment and systems during the repair of the vessel.

rows: Combination of deficiencies, that together indicates that the maintenance system is not efficiently implemented.Poor condition of hull, main deck, closing appliances, railings, ladders.Defective/missing fire-fighting and life-saving appliances, oil-pollution prevention equipment.Leaks and oil in Engine Room (E/R) spaces, pump rooms etc.Deficiencies relating to technical condition, which may lead to the limitation, suspension or withdrawal of a Class or Statutory Certificate.

Accidents and hazardous occurrences not reported to the company.Crew not able to satisfactorily conduct practical demonstrations of shipboard operations, such as starting the fire pump, emergency generator, lifeboat engine, etc.Crew not able to communicate effectively in the execution of their duties.Inability of crew to perform satisfactorily mandatory drills such as fire drills, LSA drills, pollution prevention drills, etc.

Expired classification or statutory certificates not endorsed as required.Overdue surveys, overdue audits or overdue conditions of class.Document of Compliance (DOC) not valid or not relevant to ship type.Safety Management Certificate (SMC) has incorrect data or endorsements missing.Emergency response plans and relevant SOPEP manual not available (contact points ashore shall be current and corresponding with Company name/address on DOC and SMC).Entries for relevant drills etc. in Log Book not completed in accordance with mandatory requirements.Incorrect or missing entries in the Oil Record Book.Fire Plan not up to date.

Ship's complement not complying with the Minimum Safe Manning Certificate.Master, officers, and ratings not certified as required by the STCW Convention.Serious deficiencies in respect to housekeeping and maintenance of galley, crew accommodation, and provisions stores.Port State or Flag State detentions if no evidence available, that the organization responsible for the SMS audit of the ship as specified in the Continuous Synopsis Record has been notified.

Notes:Deficiencies shall not be reported, when:they are considered normal wear and tear for the ship type and age; deficiencies, previously identified by the Company or the crew, the parties concerned are informed appropriately, there is evidence that deficiencies are being dealt with adequately by the Company including the personnel working on board.The following shall not be reported if:Port State or Flag State detentions if evidence available, that the organization responsible for the SMS audit of the ship has been notified, and additional survey for the ship's compliance with the ISM Code requirements shall be provided; the auditor of the organization responsible for the SMS audit of the ship is on board, is present in order to perform the audit and he was notified of the deficiencies identified by the surveyor. These data shall be entered in the relevant part of the Survey's Status.

Performance of Ship Security DutiesDuties of ship personnel assigned responsibilities and other shipboard personnel:-Ship is at prescribed security level at port-General physical tour onboard and at restricted areas to observe security provisions in place-Shipboard personnel attentive to security matters indicating efforts being taken to ensure appropriate security measures are in place
Crew able to identify the SSO & CSO?

Controlling access to the ShipMeasures to prevent unauthorized access to ship :-Security personnel require personal identification and reason to board (1)-All visitors are logged?-Access points identified/manned to prevent unauthorized access (1)-Unattended spaces adjoining spaces accessible to passengers/visitors are secured (1)-Security personnel appear to be briefed on :threats, suspicious persons, objects or activities and need for vigilance (1)-Security personnel patrolling deck area (2)-Access points to ship are limited (2)-Waterside access to ship deterred (2)-Restricted zone established on shore side of ship (2)-Visitors receive escort (2)-Full or partial search of ship conducted (2)-Access restricted to a single point (3)-Access to ship limited to security personnel (3)-Directing persons on board (3)-Suspend embarkation/debarkation or evacuate ship (3)-Suspend cargo operation (3)-Move the ship to a more secure area (3)-Preparations taken for a full or partial search of the ship (3)
Is personnel identification system used (1/2/3)? (Crew ID)

Controlling embarkation of persons and their effectsMeasures to prevent unauthorized weapons, dangerous substances and devices from being taken onboard :-secure areas to search persons, baggage, etc is provided (1)-checked persons/baggage segregated from unchecked (1)-embarking persons segregated from disembarking (1)-Ro-Ro/Ferries-vehicle searches performed (1)-Unaccompanied baggage screened/searched (1)-Frequency and detail of searches (persons, effects, vehicles) increased (2)-Unaccompanied baggage 100% x-ray searched (2)-Unaccompanied baggage, through x-ray or refusal to accept (3)

Monitoring deck areas and areas surrounding shipMonitor security of the ship:-Mix of lighting, watch keepers, security guards, security equipment used to observe the ship in general (1)-Stepped up use of lighting, watch keepers, security guards, security equipment (2)-Maximize use of lighting, watch keepers, security guards, security equipment (3)

Monitoring restricted areas ensuring only authorized persons have accessRestricted areas monitored / measures to prevent unauthorized access (e.g. bridge, engine room, steering compartment, cargo control room, pump room, cargo spaces, CO 2 room)Surveillance equipment in use (1)Are watch-rest hours tables posted showing the security watches & patrols of the crew assigned such duties (1)Locked/secured/roving guard to access points (1)Intrusion alarms devices in use (1)Additional restricted areas established adjacent to access points (2)Are watch-rest hours tables posted showing the security watches & patrols of the crew assigned such duties (2)Continuous use of surveillance equipment (2)Added guards for access points (2)Restricted areas established in proximity to security incidents (3)Restricted areas searched (3)

Supervision of cargo and ship’s storesProcedures for security of cargo & stores and for cargo/stores operations:-Cargo/transport units, and cargo spaces routinely checked before operations (1)-Cargo checked for match to cargo documentation (1)-Vehicles routinely searched prior loading (1)-Anti-tamper seals/methods checked (1)-Cargo visually/physically examined (1)-Scanning equipment / dogs are used (1)-Stores / provisions checked to match order prior loading (1)-Stores stowed immediately (1)-Cargo/transport units, and cargo spaces routinely checked in detail before operations (2)-Intensified checks that only intended cargo is loaded (2)-Vehicles searched intensively prior loading (2)-Anti-tamper seals/methods checked with greater frequency (2)-Cargo visually/physically examined with greater frequency (2)-Scanning equipment / dogs are used with greater frequency (2)-Enhanced security measures with shipper/responsible party law an established agreement (2)-Stores more extensively checked for match order prior to loading (2)-Cargo loading/unloading suspended (3)-Verifying the inventory of dangerous /hazardous goods and their location (if any) (3)-Stores more intensively checked, suspended, or refusal to accept (3)

Security communication is availableProcedures and equipment for communicating responses to security threats and communicating with port, port State and flag State: -Security personnel have ready access to communications (ship-to-flag, ship-to-shore, SSO to security personnel)

items.item: General requirements
Does the company have valid state license for crewing activity by the appropriate national authority?
Does the company operate on the basis of a quality management system in line with standard ISO 9001?
Has the Crewing Company Policy been developed?
Have the aims of crewing services been defined?
MLC representative of the company is mentioned:
Requirements for crewing services
Have the requirements for organization chart, work organization and Company resources influencing on provided services been defined?
Have the requirements for professional preparation, competency and condition of crew health been defined?
Have the requirements for social protection of sailors and their families been defined?
Requirements for documentation

Does the organization chart of the Company foresee such system of responsibility, management and interrelation of personnel that provides the required quality of provided services and quality control of these services?

Does the Company have the following normative documentation:- International Convention on Standards of Training, Certification and Watch keeping for Seafarers 78/95- Maritime Labor Convention, 2006?- Other national legislative documents in respect of manning services?
Does the Company follow the changes in national and international legislation?

Does the Company have and keep updated the following procedures?- crewing and manning procedure- procedure of keeping personal data of crewmembers applying to the Company- procedure of crewmembers training before the vessel’s embarkation - seafarers’ complaint procedure- procedure for cooperation with ship owner- procedure for organization of communication with crewmembers and actions in emergency situations- procedure of seafarers’ insurance

Several labour supply countries have detailed legislative provisions for their recruitment and placement services licensing regime on their official websites. The company must demonstrate knowledge on the relevant legislation
Is the order of preparation, consideration, approval, issuance and distribution of documentation followed?
Is there restricted access for documentation with respect to personal data confidentiality principles?
Are the following procedures kept in respect of:identification?keeping?definition of keeping terms?withdrawal?
Resources and personnel
Does the General Manager take part in development and implementation of the Company policy?
Does the Company policy comply with the aims of the Company?
Are the Company personnel familiarized with Company policy?
Is the Company policy analyzed periodically?
Is the company (not) operate blacklists?
Interrelations with a ship owner
Has the crewing services agreement been concluded between the Company and the ship owner or his agent?
Has the agreement been prepared in a language mutually understood and in English?

Does the agreement include the following clauses: financial aspect, order of termination and prolongation of the agreement, procedures in case of non-execution of their obligations by contracting parties, arbitration and applicable legislation?
Does the agreement define the obligations and responsibility of the ship owner including the amount of fee paying to the Company?
Has the ship owner provided the copy of collective agreement to the interested parties?
does the company have a list of customers to approach for feedback?
Procedure for development, approval, conclusion of the seafarers’ employment agreement
When concluding the seafarers’ employment agreement, is the standard form in accordance with acting national legislation used?
Is the form of agreement approved by the Company?
Is the agreement composed in English and in a language understood by the crew, if other than English?
Are the seafarers informed about their rights and obligations stated in employment agreements, before being employed or in the process of employment?
Are the relevant conditions given to the seafarers for familiarization with their employment agreements and receiving the copy of employment agreement for consultations?
Procedure for recruitment and checking of knowledge of crew members
Does the Company provide that each vessel is manned with qualified, educated and medically fit seafarers in accordance with international and national requirements?
Are the records about relevant education, preparation, skills and seafarers’ experience kept?
Are the applicants being interviewed, tested and got instructions with the following registration of results?
Does the Company have the procedures providing the relevant familiarization of new personnel and persons receiving new assignments connected with safety and environment prevention pollution?
Have the main instructions for crew members before the vessel’s embarkation been defined and exist in the Company?
Do the crewmembers familiarize with safety management system of the vessel before embarkation?
Procedure for keeping personal records and register of seafarers
Does the Company have procedures on organization of keeping personal records and register of seafarers?
Does this procedure foresee keeping of confidential information received by the Company during preparation of the seafarers’ employment agreement and its non-disclosure to the third persons?
Are the copies of seafarer’s certificates received during conclusion of the contract kept in personal records?
Procedure for organization of connection with crew and actions in emergency situations
Is the register of seafarers being sent to the vessel or applied to the Company kept in paper or electronic format?
Does the company validate certificates held by candidates being offered up?
The company must be capable to handle data responsibly and comply with respective data protection laws such as General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) in Europe
Did the Company define the channels of connection and information exchange between crewing manager and ship owner when transferring the incident message or in emergency case?
Does the Company have Emergency plan?

Does the Plan include the following:- Emergency staff and responsibilities of each member?- Procedure of gathering of emergency staff?- Procedure of establishing connection between crewing manager and ship owner?- Procedure for receiving the exact information about the number of crew members onboard?- List of responsible people with phone numbers that must be informed in emergency situations?- Procedure of informing of crew members’ families?- List of full personal data of all persons staying onboard?
Is this plan duly amended?
Are the relevant personnel duly informed about such amendments?
Procedure for seafarers insurance and system of social security of seafarers and members of their families

Does the agreement between the Company and ship owner foresee such conditions of work and social protection of seafarers that correspond to national, international requirements and collective agreements?

Does the Company provide that the ship owner or the Company itself carry out insurance of each seafarer for the following cases:Illness, trauma, injury, death?Unemployment as result of shipwreck?Loss of property?Repatriation?
Does the Company charge seafarers with any fees for employment or other relevant services?
Does the Company have procedure for providing information and consultancy for families of crew members who are onboard?
Does the Company have a contract with insurance companies that are well familiarized with the relevant kinds of insurance and have good reputation?

rows: .the validity periods of medical certificates is complied with?Unless a shorter period is required by reason of the specific duties to be performed by the seafarer concerned or is required under STCW:A medical certificate shall be valid for a maximum period of two yearsA certification of colour vision shall be valid for a maximum period of six years.

Medical Certificates can be issued on the official language of the issuing country. If the used language is not English, the text must have an English translation, based on Section A-I/9, number 8 of the STCW 78 Convention as amended.

tables.title: Equipment Table 2
rows: The maximum duration of uninterrupted service period on board, at the end of which seafarers have the right to repatriation, shall be less than twelve (12) months.

tables.title: Equipment Table 3
rows: Health and safety and accident prevention - General
Purpose:To ensure that seafarers' work environment on board ships promotes occupational safety and health

General guidance for this section:Risk assessment required under title 4.3 also includes occupational deceases. The "living conditions" of seafarers shall also be taken intoconsideration when assessing the risks on board.
Health and safety and accident prevention - Requirements

It is the duty of the shipowner to adopt effective policies and programs related to occupational safety and health, including risk evaluation as well as training and instruction of seafarers with the purpose to prevent occupational accidents, injuries and diseases, including measures to reduce and prevent the risk of exposure to harmful levels of environmental factors and chemicals substances, as well as the risk of injuries or diseases that may arise from the use of equipment and machinery on board ships.
Shipowners have the obligation to provide seafarers with protective equipment or other accident prevention devices accompanied by provisions on the use of such equipment or protection devices.
It is the obligation of the shipowner to ensure that the machineries used on board are properly guarded and that its use without appropriate safety guards is prevented.

items.item: Checklist
Does the vessel have a valid Interim MLC or MLC certificate?(all ships over 500GT on international voyages)
Does the vessel have valid and complete DMLC Part I and Part II?
Checklist
Copy of the MLC 2006 text is available onboard?
Are there any seafarers under the age of 16?
Are there any seafarers under the age of 18 on duty or working during night hours except otherwise authorized by the Administration?
Are seafarers under the age of 18 working in activities likely to jeopardize their health and safety?
Is the cook at least 18 years of age?
Do all seafarers over the age of 18 have a medical certificate valid for a period not exceeding two years?
Do all seafarers under the age of 18 have a medical certificate valid for a period not exceeding one year?
Do all medical certificates state that the vision and hearing of seafarers is satisfactory, including colour vision where applicable?
Are colour vision certificates valid?
For seafarers with temporary permission to sail (not exceeding three months) from the Authority, are all requirements being met?
Are the medical certificates of seafarers engaged on international voyages printed in English?
Are seafarers trained or certified (in accordance with national requirements) as competent to perform their duties?
Have all seafarers successfully completed training for personal safety onboard ship?
If applicable, is the seafarer recruitment or placement service used?
Is the seafarer being charged for the use of the recruitment agency?
Is the ships’ cook trained and certified to perform their duties? (for ships with manning of more than 10 seafarers)
If less than 10 seafarers aboard, is the person whose duties include food handling, fully trained?
Does the seafarer have a seafarer’s employment agreement signed by seafarer and the shipowner or a representative of the shipowner?
Is there a copy of the seafarer’s agreement in English, including the master’s agreement, available for review by the Inspector?
Checklist
Does the seafarers’ agreement contain the following particulars as required by the Standard?
Seafarer’s full name, date of birth or age and birth place
Shipowner’s name and address
The place and date when the agreement was entered into
The capacity in which the seafarer is to be employed
The amount of the seafarer’s wages
The amount of paid leave or, where applicable, the formula used for calculating it
The termination of the agreement and the conditions thereof
The health and security protection benefits to be provided to the seafarer by the shipowner
The seafarer’s entitlement to repatriation

a seafarer’s employment agreement shall continue to have effect while a seafarer is held captive on or off the ship as a result of acts of piracy or armed robbery against ships, regardless of whether the date fixed for its expiry has passed or either party has given notice to suspend or terminate it.

wages and other entitlements, under the SEA and any Collective Bargaining Agreement (CBA), including allotments, shall continue to be paid during the entire period of captivity until the seafarer is release and repatriated in accordance with MLC 2006 requirements or dies in captivity. The date of any death in captivity will be determined in accordance with national laws and recommendations.

Seafarers, prior to or in the process of engagement, shall be informed about their rights under the seafarers’ recruitment and placement services’ system of protection, to compensate seafarers for monetary loss that they may incur as a result of the failure of the recruitment and placement service or the relevant shipowner under the seafarers’ employment agreement to meet its obligations to them.
Are seafarer’s paid at maximum intervals of a month as per their SEA or CBA?
Is there evidence of more than one set of wage accounts in use?
If a collective bargaining agreement is in place, is it valid and complete?
Are seafarers given a monthly account of payments due and amounts paid?
Are seafarers able to allot a portion of their wages and is it remitted in due time?
Is the minimum notice period mentioned in the SEA seven days or greater?
Does the crew have records of employment available?
Are records of hours of work/hours of rest kept up to date in accordance with the set national requirements?
Checklist
Do the records of hours of work/hours of rest represent actual hours worked and rested?

Is a table with the shipboard working arrangements posted in an easily accessible place and contain, for every position, the schedule of service at sea and in port, and do these arrangements conform to the applicable minimum hours of rest or maximum hours of work?
Is the ship manned according to the ship manning certificate?
Is the crew on the manning certificate carrying out tasks as per their designation, e.g. Cadets not working as Ordinary Seaman, second cook not working as first cook etc?

The location and general arrangement of the crew accommodation including the allocation of each space (cabin, mess room, alleyway, WC, etc), occupants of cabins and the disposition of furniture, fittings, the means and arrangement of ventilation, lighting, heating and the sanitary arrangements been reviewed on behalf of the government of the country of registry and checked now onboard?
Confirmation that seafarers are given shore leave when possible and no restriction to shore leave is imposed by the shipowner/master without adequate reason.
Are cabins maintained in a clean and tidy condition?
Are mess rooms maintained in a clean and tidy condition?
Is insulation in accommodation in a satisfactory condition?
Is lighting satisfactory in the accommodation?
Is drainage in the accommodation working?
Are noise and vibrations and other ambient factors satisfactory?
Is ventilation in sleeping rooms and mess rooms satisfactory?
Is the air conditioning system or heating as appropriate in satisfactory condition?
Is the ventilation in sanitary spaces satisfactory?
Are individual sleeping rooms provided for each seafarer, where applicable?
Is seafarer accommodation, clothes locker and drawers in a satisfactory condition?
Are sanitary facilities clean and in satisfactory condition?

Is a minimum of one toilet, one wash basin and one tub or shower, or both, for every six persons, who do not have personal facilities available and are these in satisfactory condition, where applicable?
Do the washbasin and/or private bathroom have hot and cold running fresh water?
Is hospital accommodation provided for ships carrying fifteen or more seafarers engaged on an international voyage or more than three days?
Checklist
Is hospital accommodation used for any other purpose?
Does the laundry contain washing and drying facilities?
Is mosquito protection available for vessels trading to mosquito infested ports?
Are recreational facilities maintained in a clean and tidy condition? Are amenities and services available at no cost to seafarers?
Is appropriate seafarers’ social connectivity available, as adapted to meet the special needs of seafarers onboard?
Are seafarers having reasonable access to ship-to-shore telephone communications and Internet access, where available, with any charges for the use of these services being reasonable in amount?
Are frequent inspections carried out and records available to ensure that seafarer accommodation is clean and in a good state of repair?
Are seafarers provided with quality, varied and nutritious meals taking into account the differing cultural and religious backgrounds – free of charge?
Are seafarers provided with adequate, varied, balanced and nutritious meals taking into account the differing cultural and religious backgrounds – free of charge?

Are regular inspections of the galley, galley equipment, supplies of food and drinking water in relation to their quantity, nutritional value, quality and variety, and storage and handling spaces carried out, and does the condition report reflect the hygienic status of the galley and its surrounds?

Are seafarers provided prompt and adequate medical care, including emergency dental care, and the right to visit a qualified medical doctor in ports of call where practicable, at no cost to the seafarer while working onboard?
Does the vessel carry a medicine chest, medical equipment and medical guide for ships (WHO) in compliance with national requirements and records of inspection maintained?
For ships on international voyages of more than three days, carrying over one hundred persons, is there a qualified onboard doctor?
For ships not required to carry a doctor, is there at least one seafarer qualified to provide first aid that meets STCW requirements?
Is the standard medical report available from the Master?
Is their evidence of implementation of health and safety national regulations?
Checklist
Is there evidence of reporting and correcting unsafe conditions and investigating and reporting onboard accidents?
Is there a ships’ safety committee onboard the vessel where there are five or more seafarers?

Has the company set us procedures or practices for eliminating shipboard harassment and bullying including processes for reporting and correcting problems arising from harassment and bullying onboard?
Does the ship have onboard procedures for the fair, effective and expeditious handling of seafarer complaints?
Does the procedure allow for the seafarer to be accompanied or represented?
Does the procedure allow for the seafarer to complain to the Master or external authorities?
Does the procedure prescribe safeguards against victimization for those making a complaint?
Does the procedure include contact information for the competent authority in the flag state?
Is a copy of the complaints procedure available for seafarers in the working language of the ship?
Financial securities in relation to repatriation of seafarers and ship-owners liabilities

In the case of reported seafarer death during the ship’s voyage, has the shipowner facilitated the repatriation of the body or ashes of the seafarer, in accordance with the wishes of the seafarer or their next of kin, as appropriate, and as soon as practicable?

items.item: Are the galley appliances operational?
Are appliances using naked flames adequately protected and secured?
Is the galley maintained in clean and efficient conditions?
Are floor drains in the galley, including water/grease traps, in good condition?
Are cold and hot drinking water available at the washing-up area?
Is fresh water available at hose connections for washing down?
In vessel over 1000 GT, is the mechanical ventilation of the galley operational?
Is the artificial lighting of the galley operational?
Are provision store rooms being used exclusively for the intended purpose?
Are provision store rooms being maintained in a clean and efficient condition?
Is the lighting of provision store rooms operational?
Are refrigerated provisions rooms being exclusively used for the intended purpose?
Are the refrigerated spaces maintained in clean and efficient conditions?
Are adequate temperatures maintained inside the refrigerated spaces?
In walk-in rooms, can doors be opened from both sides and is the alarm operational?
Is drinking water available at galleys, main pantries and adjacent to messrooms?
Are drinking and sanitary water tanks being exclusively used for the intended purpose and
intended purpose and kept in an internally clean condition?
Are fresh water evaporators and water treatment equipment, if used to supply drinking or
sanitary fresh water, operational (including automatic control systems and alarms)?
In vessel over 1000 GT, are fresh water power pumps and reserve pumps operational?
In vessel over 1000 GT, is cool drinking water available from al least one source?
Are external bulkheads and other bulkheads required to be watertight or gastight (such as those
separating the crew accommodation from machinery spaces, storerooms, etc) maintained in an
adequate condition?
Is the insulation of external bulkheads and other bulkheads which are required to be insulated
against heat properly maintained?
Is the insulation of steam and hot water pipes crossing the crew accommodation properly
maintained so as to avoid danger and discomfort to the occupants?
If cabins and messroms are painted, is the paint used of a light colour?
Are wall surfaces, floors and ceilings well maintained, and being renewed or restored when necessary?
Are cabins, messrooms, recreations rooms if provided, and public sanitary spaces properly
ventilated, without stale air, odours or an excessive temperature in relation to the outside?
Are the ventilation systems operational?
and to minimize the risk of fire?
Has the inspector verified that portable stoves are not used on board?
Are the sleeping arrangements for the crew in accordance with the ILO / MLC and in particular with the
assignment of numbers and categories of persons to each sleeping room?
Have the furnishings and particulars of each of the sleeping rooms been verified and found acceptable?
In particular, are the ventilation, heating and electric lights of each sleeping room operational?
Are messrooms and their furnishings in good state of maintenance, with numbers of seats, tables,
In particular, are the ventilation, heating and electric lights of each messroom in working order?
Are the open deck areas designated for recreation of the crew in good condition and free of
obstructions which may impede their use?
Is crew recreation accommodation in good condition, adequately furnished, ventilated, heated
and with electric lights ?
Are the public bathrooms in a good state of maintenance, with walls, floors and fixtures in working order?
Are the waste pipes and soil pipes free of obstruction and in working order, without leaks which
may create health hazards?
Are cold fresh water supplies and hot fresh water supplies, or means to heat water, in working
order in all public bathrooms?
Is fresh water available in public bathrooms at all times or if not continuously in adequate
quantities and time periods?
Is flushing water available in WC's at all times and are local controls at each unit operational?
Are private and semi-private bathrooms in good state of maintenance, with fixtures, water supplies
and drains in working order?
Are WC screens, doors and, if fitted, locks in good order?
Are floor drains in bathroom used by more than one person in working order?
Are the facilities for washing clothes details in working order?
Are the facilities for drying clothes also in working order?
If provided, is the hospital in good state of maintenance, not used for other than its intended
purpose and with its bathroom in working order?
Is the medicine chest properly maintained, with its contents valid and renewed as necessary,
and instructions available?
If the ship is of 3000 GT or more, are the deck and engine offices well maintained and used
for the intended purpose?
If the ship regularly trades to mosquito infested areas, are the screens for side scuttles, ventilators
and doors available and in good order?
If the ship regularly trades to the Tropics or the Persian Gulf, and does not have air conditioning,
are the awnings for use in exposed deck area above the crew accommodation and open deck
recreation areas available and in good order?
Are the arrangements on board adapted, as far as is reasonable and practicable, to the particular
national customs of the crew or, when several national groups are employed, are reasonable
arrangements made to cope with these differences?
Is the crew accommodation maintained in a clean and decent condition and free of goods and
stores not the property of the crew?
Are weekly Inspections conducted by the Master, or a person delegated by the Master, accompanied
by one or more members of the crew, and are their results entered in the ship's log book?

Are there instructions in the form of a medical guide on board

Are there Magnetic Compasses (for steering purpose and magnetic compass outside the bridge for use in taking bearings or azimuths, or alternatively, a single compass outside the bridge steering position with a reflector inside for steering purpose)

International Code of Signals (applicable to all vessels)
Daylight Signaling lamp was tested on main and emergency source of power and was found operational (For vessels of 45 meters in length and above)
LIFE – SAVING APPLIANCES
Is the vessel provided with, at least, 12 parachute Distress Rocket Signals capable of producing a single bright red star at a high altitude and are they still valid (Usual validity, 3 year).

Is the vessel provided with a Line Throwing Appliance with 2 lines and 2 projectiles, with valid rockets, capable of Throwing a line over a minimum distance of 230 m (70.12 Ft = 250 yards) (applicable to all ships)

Are the lifeboat’s sizes in accordance with the (minimum Length = 4.9 m = 16 Ft)
Are Lifeboats and/or Liferafts of an approved type
Are their Launching-gear of an approved design and construction complying with the applicable requirement of SOLAS according to the date of construction
Are the skates or other suitable means provided to facilitate launching

LIFEBOATS / RESCUE BOATS:
Lifeboats now turned out and lowered to embarkation deck
Lifeboats now turned out and lowered to water

Lifeboats marked with dimensions, name, port or registry and number of persons

AVAILABILITY OF LIFEBOAT AND LIFERAFTS:

Detail Instructions for operating Lifeboats are conspicuously posted
Are Lifeboats or Rescue Boats properly marked (on both bows) with the name of the parent ship, port of registry, principal dimensions and Carrying Capacity
DAVITS FOR LIFEBOATS AND LIFERAFTS:

Hidden parts of wire falls have been inspected
EMBARKATION INTO AND ILLUMINATION OF LIFEBOATS/ LIFERAFTS:
Does the vessels has a rope ladder for each set of davits capable of reaching light water line
Suitable lights for illuminating Liferafts and/ or Lifeboats are provided tested with the emergency source of power and found in good order

Boat lashings and release arrangements in order

Embarkation ladders checked

Alarm system tested

LIFERAFTS:

No.

Lifejackets readily accessible and their position plainly indicated

items.item: Does the number of the seafarers serving on board is in compliance with Minimum Safe Manning Certificate issued by the Administration?
Do the master and officers hold valid Certificates of Competency as required by the Minimum Safe Manning Document?
Do seafarers hold relevant certificates of proficiency (COP) or documentary evidences?
Do seafarers on board hold valid medical certificates?
Validity of the ship’s statutory certificates and class status have been confirmed.
Confirm there are no pending class conditions.
Confirm there are no pending Flag/PSC recommendations/deficiencies.
Ship specific manuals and booklets are on board, approved and up to date as applicable including:
Life Saving Appliances and Fire Safety Training manuals
Trim and stability booklet
Loading manual
Cargo securing manual
Survey planning document (including enhanced survey report files) - required by ESP oil tankers, bulk carriers and chemical carriers
Shipboard Oil Pollution Emergency Plan (SOPEP) including updated contact list
Shipboard Marine Pollution Emergency Plan (SMPEP)
Ship Energy Efficiency Management Plan (SEEMP)
Part II / Part III (GT>5.000)
Fire Control Plan
Grain loading manual
Damage control plan
Loading instrument (Oil Tankers, Bulk Carriers >120m)
Coating technical file - required for Performance Standard for Protective Coatings (PSPC)
Emergency towing procedure
Oil Record Book
Garbage Record Book
Ballast Water Management Plan
Ballast Water Record Book
Applicable maintenance manuals
Ship’s side shell plates without damage and excessive wastage, as far as visible.
Bulwarks, handrails and guardrails, cat walks without damage and maintained in proper condition.
No excessive deformation, corrosion, cracking, buckling on deck and hull frames.
Watertight and weathertight doors are in good condition and are closing properly without any obstructions.
Ventilators, air pipes and closure devices are checked in good condition without holes, wastage or excessive corrosion.
Confirm that the deck line and load line mark are properly positioned.
Cargo hold / tanks or other hatchway coamings in proper condition. No excessive corrosion, wastage, deformation or cracking found.

Cargo hold hatch covers (or other hatchways), cargo tanks’ lids and weathertight closing appliances are in proper working condition and have been checked for missing or damaged gaskets, cleats, wedges and securing devices. No excessive corrosion or wastage is found anywhere.
Watertight and weathertight doors are in good condition and are closing properly without any obstructions.
Ventilators, air pipes and closure devices are checked in good condition without holes, wastage or excessive corrosion.
Magnetic compass readable, free of air bubbles. The deviation card has been updated. Compass correction log is available on board.
Navigation lights in proper working condition. No damages or corrosion found.
The signaling lamp is in good working condition and has been tested on both emergency power supply and battery power.
Nautical charts and publications available and updated for each intended voyage.
Navigational log book is available and relative entries are being made.
The vessel’s automatic identification system (AIS) is properly programmed and operational.
VDR/ S-VDR operates properly and annual performance test certificate is on board.
LRIT is in good working condition, and the test report is available.
EPIRB is in good working condition (annual test report and shore-based maintenance are valid).
Voyage passage planning is carried out from berth to berth, signed by Nav. officers and the Captain.
BNWAS is in working condition, also to demonstrate that system is protected by security pass code (should be under control of Master).
Radio equipment is in good working order and is serviced and tested by a recognized radio technician.
Crew personnel were able to demonstrate VHF/ MF/ HF test call to shore station or other ship.
GMDSS Shore-Based Maintenance Agreement is up to date and valid.
NAVTEX and printer in proper working condition.
Mooring and anchoring equipment and fittings (anchors, chains, winch, windlasses, ropes, bitts, etc.) are in good working condition and ready for safe use by the crew. No damages.
A-60 Insulation is intact in all areas (emergency escape trunks, steering gear room etc.).
Fire line isolating valve(s) between the engine room (ER) and deck has been tested to work properly and is/are clearly marked.
Fire doors in good condition, properly closing and not purposely open (i.e. tied back with “hooks”).
Fire detection arrangements working properly. Fire detection panel displays with no faults. Fire, smoke and heat detectors have been tested for proper operation.
Heat detector testing device is available on board.
Portable and fixed firefighting systems have been serviced as required, and extinguishers are properly marked with date of servicing.
Fixed firefighting systems have been serviced and do not have any loose hoses, and the system has been reactivated.
Foam systems where fit have had analysis samples taken and are operation-ready.
Fire main is in good condition and does not have patches or holes. No leaks.
Fire hoses are not leaking and have been checked for dry rot and usability.

The main and emergency fire pumps are to be in proper working condition. They are capable of taking sea suction and maintaining the proper line pressure. Can each fire pump deliver at least the two required jets of water?
Quick closing valves are in good condition and operating properly.
All fire dampers properly working and closing, corrosion-free. Indication of damper flap position (OPEN-CLOSE) is marked clearly.
Main and auxiliary engine fuel oil leakage alarms are working and drain valves are in closed position.
Means of escape and access are not obstructed.

Fire control plans are up to date, readable and with appropriate IMO markings and symbols. A duplicate set of fire control plans is properly maintained in weathertight enclosure outside the deckhouse for the assistance of shore-side fire-fighting personnel.
Confirm that emergency instructions are available for each person on board. Muster list is posted in conspicuous places and they are in a language understood by the persons on board.
Crew is able to satisfactorily demonstrate emergency fire drill.
All key crew members communicate effectively during the drill and are familiar with their assigned duties and with the operation control of emergency equipment?
All necessary emergency equipment is available and in good condition during the drill.
Emergency Escape Breathing Devices (EEBD) are available and with a valid annual inspection certificate.
General and fire alarm audible throughout accommodation and crew working spaces.
Alarm Monitoring System in ER in proper working condition.

The lifeboat (rescue boat) structure (hull integrity, seats/ thwarts, flooring, releasing hook connections to the boat, releasing gear, tiller/ gudgeons) has been checked for proper maintenance with no damage, wastage or rot.
The lifeboat (rescue boat) engine is in good working condition and has been operationally tested, and fuel tank is full.
The lifeboat (rescue boat) equipment has been checked for proper quantity, expiration date and condition.
Lifeboats (rescue boat) have been lowered as per schedule and released from hooks to confirm release mechanisms.
Is the lifeboat’s (rescue boat's) davit annual and 5-yearly inspection certificate available from an approved servicing station?
Liferafts have been serviced by an approved servicing company. Certificate not expired.
Liferafts are properly secured. Hydrostatic release units are correctly connected and have valid service certificates and/or expiry dates.
Launching arrangements are in good condition with no obstructions.

Davits were checked for wastage, proper hoisting/lowering and braking function. Sheaves and loose gear are not worn. Wires have been serviced and changed out as necessary. Limit switches and winches have been tested.
Distress flares are available on board and not expired.

Embarkation ladders for survival craft is in good condition and ready to use. They should extend, in a single length, from the deck to the waterline in the lightest seagoing condition under all conditions of trim of up to 10° and a list of up to 20° either way.
Line-throwing appliance(s) is/are available on board and rockets not expired.

Lifebuoys (type approved) – the correct number and type (with line, light or smoke) is available and with legible vessel markings. They are properly stowed according safety plan and ready to use without obstructions.
Lifejackets (type approved) – the correct number is available on board and stowed according the safety plan.
Immersion suits – the correct number is available on board with a valid annual inspection.
Training manual and on-board training aids for life saving appliances are available on board in crew’s working language.
Are the latest revisions of the SMS manuals, procedures and records are readily available at relevant locations?
Are the name and address of the company match 100% with the entries on DOC and SMC?
Declaration of Company operating the vessel and Declaration of DPA have been submitted to the flag Administration, if required.
Can senior officers identify the 'designated person" responsible for the operation of the ship and the means to contact that person?
Is relevant documentation regarding the SMS in a working language or languages understood by the ship's personnel?
Does the ship's SMS have an effective maintenance plan which includes the testing of stand by equipment and critical equipment/systems and are records available?
Are programmes for drills and exercises to prepare for emergency actions available on board and are records available?
Are the crew members able to communicate effectively in the execution of their duties related to the SMS?
Safety induction, shipboard familiarization and safety training of crew have been carried out as per the SMS
Have the procedures to report non-conformities, accidents and hazardous occurrences been followed?
Internal audits have been carried out at intervals not exceeding 12 months by auditors who are independent of areas audited.
External audits have been carried out as required by the ISM Code.
Audit reports are available on board.
Timely corrective action has been taken to close out audit findings and records are maintained.
Following a PSC detention, corrective actions taken by the ship must were not limited to the PSC deficiencies
Master, Ship Security Officer (SSO) and crew members are aware of all levels of ship security and applicable procedures at each level as well as their security duties.
Ship Security Plan (SSP) is protected and unauthorized access prevented.

Access to ship is controlled and crew members on watch are familiar with the access control measures at each security level. This includes control measures applied at ladders, gangways, ramps, doors, side scuttles, windows, ports, cranes, hoisting gears, etc. as applicable.
Restricted areas have been identified and crew members are aware of access control measures applied to these areas.
Security equipment maintained and tested as necessary, including the SSAS 1.
Shipboard security training and drills are periodically carried out in accordance with SSP. Relevant records are maintained on board.
Declaration of Security (DOS) for last 10 port calls are maintained on board.
Full record of Continuous Synopsis Record (CSR) properly updated is maintained on board.
Seafarers Employment Agreement signed by the seafarer and the MLC shipowner (or authorized representative) available on board (incl. CBA if applicable).
Valid training, medical and competency certificates of seafarers available on board.
Wages paid in accordance with the SEA and individual monthly statements available on board.
Correctly maintained records of hours of work and rest available and signed by the seafarer and the Master.
Accommodation and sanitary facilities are functional and adequate for number of personnel onboard.
Accommodations, corridors, cabins, decks, cold room, dining rooms, galley are maintained in clean/hygienic condition.
Heating, lighting, ventilation systems, and other fittings and fixtures are maintained in clean and good working condition.
Food and drinking water of adequate quantity, nutrition and quality are provided.
Medical chest, medical supplies and equipment meets national requirements and have not expired.
Lighting in all messrooms, recreation spaces and sleeping rooms is in proper working condition.
Machinery, boilers and other pressure vessels, associated piping system and fittings, and rotating machinery parts are protected as to reduce any danger to persons on board.
Proper earth fault insulation is provided in electrical switchboards.
Engine room (general cleaning)
Main engine (also photograph leaks and visible damage, if any)
Main generators (also photograph visible leaks and damage, if any)
Emergency generator (also photograph leaks and visible damage, if any)
Sewage treatment plant (general condition and alarms)
Incinerator (ignition evidence)
Purifiers (cleanliness condition of the area where they are located)
OWS & Dump Valve
Photos of tests of the alarm panel in the control room of bilge machines, leaks in generators and main machine when practicable
Maneuvering system
Emergency fire pump and photos of hoses that show the pressure
Engine fans (opening and closing mechanism and its condition).
Smoke detector test & the equipment used for the test
Lifeboats and rescue boat and their engines
Condition of cargo holds hatch covers and their closing mechanisms in Cargo Ships
Freeboard mark
ISM – photos of the NCR closed by the company in case the ship has reports of PSC with Deficiencies or Detentions
Evidence of the inspections carried out on the fire-fighting and rescue devices ashore by companies authorized by the RO or the Administration

items.item: ANNUAL SURVEY
Examination of current certificates and other records for oil pollution prevention:

checking the validity, as appropriate, of the Cargo Ship Safety Equipment Certificate, the Cargo Ship Safety Radio Certificate and the Cargo Ship Safety Construction Certificate or the Cargo Ship Safety Certificate
checking the validity of the International Load Line Certificate or International Load Line Exemption Certificate
checking the validity of the International Oil Pollution Prevention Certificate
checking the certificates of class, if the ship is classed with a classification society
checking, when appropriate, the validity of the International Sewage Pollution Prevention Certificate

checking, when appropriate, the validity of the International Certificate of Fitness for the Carriage of Dangerous Chemicals in Bulk or the Certificate of Fitness for the Carriage of Dangerous Chemicals in Bulk
checking, when appropriate, the validity of the International Certificate of Fitness for the Carriage of Liquefied Gases in Bulk

checking the certificates for the type approval of the oil pollution prevention equipment, such as the oil content meters and oil/water interface detectors, and sighting the records of the various oil discharge monitoring equipment, as applicable (MARPOL 90/04 Annex I reg.31)

Survey for the oil pollution prevention:

examining externally the oil filtering equipment and confirming, as far as practicable, its satisfactory operation including, when appropriate, testing the operation of the automatic means provided to stop the discharge of effluent and the alarm for the oil filtering equipment (MARPOL 90/04/15 Annex I regs.14 and 15)
testing, where fitted, the oil filtering equipment required for discharge in special areas (MARPOL 90/04/15 Annex I reg.15)

confirming the segregation of oil fuel and water ballast systems and that the arrangements prohibit the carriage of oil in forepeak tanks or in spaces forward of the collision bulkhead (MARPOL 90/04 Annex I reg.16)

checking that the arrangement of oil residue (sludge) tank and its discharge arrangements are satisfactory and confirming that, where applicable, homogenizers, sludge incinerators or other recognized means for the control of sludge are satisfactory (MARPOL 90/04/15 Annex I reg.12, and where applicable, Polar Code part II-A/para.1.2.4)
confirming that a standard discharge connection is provided (MARPOL 90/04 Annex I reg.13)
Survey for the oil pollution prevention (the additional requirements for oil tankers)
examining the oil discharge monitoring and control system and its associated equipment (MARPOL 90/04 Annex I reg.31) and, in particular:
examining externally the system and equipment and, if applicable, verifying that the instrument is properly sealed

confirming, as far as practicable, the satisfactory operation of the oil discharge monitoring and control system including the oil content meter and, where applicable, the automatic and manual means provided to stop the discharge of effluent and the starting interlock
observing that indicators and recording devices are operable and verifying that a sufficient supply of consumables for the recorders is on board
testing, as far as practicable, any audible or visual alarms fitted to the oil discharge monitoring and control system
examining, as far as practicable, the oil/water interface detectors (MARPOL 90/04 Annex I reg.32)
confirming that no cross-connections have been fitted between the cargo and segregated ballast systems (MARPOL 90/04 Annex I reg.18)

where a portable spool piece is provided for the emergency discharge of segregated ballast by connecting the segregated ballast system to a cargo pump, confirming that non-return valves are fitted on the segregated ballast connections and that the spool piece is mounted in a conspicuous position in the pump-room with a permanent notice restricting its use (MARPOL 90/04 Annex I reg.18)
confirming by sighting that there has been no contamination with oil in the segregated ballast tanks (MARPOL 90/04 Annex I reg.18)
confirming, as far as practicable, that the dedicated clean ballast tank arrangement remains satisfactory (MARPOL 90/04 Annex I reg.18)
confirming by sighting that there has been no contamination with oil in the dedicated clean ballast tanks (MARPOL 90/04 Annex I reg.18)
confirming, as far as practicable, that the crude oil washing system remains satisfactory (MARPOL 90/04 Annex I reg.33) and, in particular:

examining externally the crude oil washing piping, pumps, valves and deck-mounted washing machines for signs of leakage and checking that all anchoring devices for crude oil washing piping are intact and secure
confirming, in those cases where drive units are not integral with the tank cleaning machines, that the number of operational drive units as specified in the Manual are on board
checking that, when fitted, steam heaters for water washing can be properly isolated during crude oil washing operations, either by double shut-off valves or clearly identifiable blanks
checking that the prescribed means of communications between the deck watchkeeper and the cargo control position is operational
confirming that an overpressure relief device (or other approved arrangement) is fitted to the pumps supplying the crude oil washing systems
confirming that flexible hoses for the supply of oil to the washing machines on combination carriers are of an approved type, are properly stored and are in good condition
verifying, where applicable and as far as practicable, the effectiveness of the crude oil washing system (MARPOL 90/04 Annex I reg.33) and, in particular:
checking tanks containing departure and/or arrival ballast water, as applicable, to confirm the effectiveness of the cleaning and stripping

checking, as far as practicable, that the crude oil washing machines are operable and, when the survey is carried out during crude oil washing operations, observing the proper operation of the washing machines by means of the movement indicators and/or sound patterns or other approved methods
checking, as far as practicable, the effectiveness of the stripping system in appropriate cargo tanks by observing the monitoring equipment and by hand dipping or other approved means
confirming that on those existing tankers operating with special ballast arrangements the arrangements are as approved and are satisfactory (MARPOL 90/04 Annex I reg.18)

confirming, as appropriate and as far as practicable, that the arrangements for the prevention of oil pollution in the event of collision or stranding are approved and are satisfactory (MARPOL 90/04/14 Annex I regs.19 to 22, Polar Code part II-A/paras. 1.2.2 and 1.2.3)
examining the piping systems associated with the discharge of dirty ballast or oil contaminated water including the part flow system, if fitted (MARPOL 90/04 Annex I reg.30)
testing the communication system between the observation and discharge control positions (MARPOL 90/04 Annex I reg.30)
examining the means of draining cargo pumps and cargo lines, including the stripping device and the connections for pumping to the slop or cargo tanks or ashore (MARPOL 90/04 Annex I reg.30)

confirming for oil tankers of 5,000 tonnes deadweight and above that arrangements are in place to provide prompt access to shore-based damage stability and residual structural strength computerized calculation programs (MARPOL 90/04 Annex I reg.37.4)

checking of the items as specified for annual survey in section 1.1 of this Annex to the check-list

checking of the items as specified for annual survey in section 1.2 of this Annex to the check-list

checking of the items as specified for annual survey in section 1.3 of this Annex to the check-list

examining the oily-water separating equipment or oil filtering equipment or process unit, where fitted, including associated pumps, piping and fittings for wear and corrosion (MARPOL 90/04/15 Annex I regs.14 and 15)

examining the oil content meter (15 ppm alarm and bilge monitor) for obvious defects, deterioration or damage and checking the record of calibration of the meter when done in accordance with the manufacturer's operational and instruction manual (MARPOL 90/04/15 Annex I reg.14)

checking of the items as specified for annual survey in section 1.4 of this Annex to the check-list

examining the oil discharge monitoring and control system and the oil content meter for obvious defects, deterioration or damage, and checking the record of calibration of the meter when done in accordance with the manufacturer's operational and instruction manual (MARPOL 90/04 Annex I reg.31)
confirming the satisfactory operation of the oil/water interface detectors (MARPOL 90/04 Annex I reg.32);
for the crude oil washing system (MARPOL 90/04 Annex I reg.33):

examining the crude oil washing piping outside the cargo tanks; if upon examination there is any doubt as to its condition, the piping may be required to be pressure tested, gauged or both; particular attention should be paid to any repairs such as welded doublers
confirming the satisfactory operation of the isolation valves to steam heaters for washing water, when fitted

examining at least two selected cargo tanks for the express purpose of verifying the continued effectiveness of the installed crude oil washing and stripping systems; if a tank cannot be gas freed for the safe entry of the surveyor, an internal examination of it should not be conducted, in which case the verification is to be made by another alternative method acceptable to the Administration; an acceptable alternative would be satisfactory results during the surveys required by (OA) 1.2.4.9 (MARPOL 90/04 Annex I reg.33)
examining the manual and/or remote operation of the individual tank valves (or other similar closing devices) to be kept closed at sea (MARPOL 90/04 Annex I regs.23 and 26)
Examination of current certificates and other records for oil pollution prevention
checking of the items as specified for annual survey in section 1.1 of this Annex to the check-list except the validity of the International Oil Pollution Prevention Certificate

verifying that, if applicable, the 15 ppm bilge alarm has been calibrated by the manufacturer or a person authorized by the manufacturer and that a valid calibration certificate is available on board *)*) - Applicable for installations complying with the Revised guidelines and specifications for pollution prevention equipment for machinery space bilges of ships (resolution MEPC.107(49)), as amended

checking of the items as specified for annual survey in section 1.2 of this Annex the check-list

checking of the items as specified for intermediate survey in section 2.3 of this Annex to the check-list
confirming, if necessary by simulated test or equivalent, the satisfactory operation of the oily-water separating equipment or oil filtering equipment (MARPOL 90/04/15 Annex I reg.14)

confirming, if necessary by simulated test or equivalent, the satisfactory operation of the oil discharge monitoring and control system, including where practicable the automatic and manual operation of the means provided to stop the discharge of effluent (MARPOL 90/04 Annex I reg.31)
confirming the satisfactory operation of the alarm for the oil filtering system (MARPOL 90/04/15 Annex I reg.14)

confirming the satisfactory operation of homogenizers, sludge incinerators or other recognized means for the control of sludge when the size of oil residue (sludge) tank is approved on the basis of such installations (MARPOL 90/04/15 Annex I reg.12)
Survey for the oil pollution prevention (the additional requirements for oil tankers)
checking of the items as specified for intermediate survey in section 2.4 of this Annex to the check-list
confirming that the arrangements of slop tanks or cargo tanks designated as slop tanks and associated piping systems are satisfactory (MARPOL 90/04/15 Annex I regs.29 and 34)

confirming, if necessary by simulated test or equivalent, the satisfactory operation of the oil discharge monitoring and control system and its associated equipment, including the oil/water interface detectors (MARPOL 90/04 Annex I regs.31 and 32)
confirming that the arrangements of pumps, pipes and valves are in accordance with the requirements for SBT systems (MARPOL 90/04 Annex I reg.18)
confirming that the arrangements of pumps, pipes and valves are in accordance with the Revised specifications for oil tankers with dedicated clean ballast tanks (MARPOL 90/04 Annex I reg.18)
confirming that the crude oil washing system is in accordance with the requirements for such systems (MARPOL 90/04 Annex I reg.33) and, in particular:
carrying out pressure testing of the crude oil washing system to at least the working pressure
examining the cargo tanks for the express purpose of verifying the continued effectiveness of the installed crude oil washing and stripping systems
examining internally, when fitted, the isolation valves for any steam heaters

verifying, by internal tank inspection or by another alternative method acceptable to the Administration, the effectiveness of the crude oil washing system; if the tank cannot be gas freed for the safe entry of the surveyor, an internal inspection should not be conducted; an acceptable alternative would be satisfactory results during the surveys required by (OA) 1.2.4.9 (MARPOL 90/04 Annex I reg.33)
confirming that there is no leakage from those ballast pipelines passing through cargo tanks and those cargo pipelines passing through ballast tanks (MARPOL 90/04 Annex I regs.18 and 33)
confirming that the pumping, piping and discharge arrangements are satisfactory (MARPOL 90/04 Annex I reg.30) and, in particular:
confirming that the piping systems associated with the discharge of dirty ballast water or oil contaminated water are satisfactory
confirming that the means of draining cargo pumps and cargo lines, including the stripping device and the connections for pumping to the slop or cargo tanks or ashore are satisfactory
confirming that the arrangements for the part flow system, where fitted, are satisfactory;
confirming that closing devices installed in the cargo transfer system and cargo piping as appropriate are satisfactory (MARPOL 90/04 Annex I regs.23 and 26)

confirming, as appropriate and as far as practicable, that the arrangements for the prevention of oil pollution in the event of collision or stranding are satisfactory (MARPOL 90/04/14 Annex I regs.19 to 22; Polar Code part II-A/paras.1.2.2 and 1.2.3)

confirming for oil tankers of 5,000 tonnes deadweight and above that arrangements are in place to provide prompt access to shore-based damage stability and residual structural strength computerized calculation programs (MARPOL 90/04 Annex I reg.37.4)

if the engine parameter check method is used:

review engine documentation contained in the technical file and the record book or an electronic record book *) of engine parameters to check, as far as practicable, engine rating, duty and limitation/restrictions as given in the technical file;*) - Refer to the Guidelines for the use of electronic record books under MARPOL (resolution MEPC.312(74)).

conduct survey as detailed in the technical file.

tables.title: Table 1 Application of the requirements to different modes of carriage of dangerous goods in ships and cargo spaces
headers: Where X appears in table 1 it means this requirement is applicable to all classes of dangerous goods as given in the appropriate line of table 3, except as indicated by the notes.
rows: For application of requirements of regulation 19 to different classes of dangerous goods, see table 2

Notes:1.For classes 4 and 5.1 solids not applicable to closed freight containers. For classes 2, 3, 6.1 and when carried in closed freight containers, the ventilation rate may be reduced to not less than two air changes per hour. For classes 4 and 5.1 liquids when carried in closed freight containers, the ventilation rate may be reduced to not less than two air changes per hour. For the purpose of this requirement, a portable tank is a closed freight container2.Applicable to decks only3.Applies only to closed RO-RO spaces, not capable of being sealed.4.In the special case where the barges are capable of containing flammable vapours or alternatively if they are capable of discharging flammable vapours to a safe space outside the barge carrier compartment by means of ventilation ducts connected to the barges, these requirements may be reduced or waived to the satisfaction of the Administration.5.Special category spaces shall be treated as closed RO-RO spaces when dangerous goods are carried.
Table 2 Application of the requirements to different classes of dangerous goods for ships and cargo spaces carrying solid dangerous goods in bulk

Notes:6.The hazards of substances in this class which may be carried in bulk are such that special consideration must be given by the Administration to the construction and equipment of the ship involved in addition to meeting the requirements enumerated in this table.7.Only applicable to Seedcake containing solvent extractions, to Ammonium nitrate and to Ammonium nitrate fertilizers.8.Only applicable to Ammonium nitrate and to Ammonium nitrate fertilizers. However, a degree of protection in accordance with standards contained in the International Electrotechnical Commission publication 60079, Electrical Apparatus for Explosive Gas Atmospheres, is sufficient.9.Only suitable wire mesh guards are required.10.The requirements of the International Maritime Solid Bulk Cargoes (IMSBC) Code, as amended, are sufficient.
